rationales.self_direction: "I just sat there" while Pieter took over his proposal, showing he let someone else dictate the moment instead of asserting his own voice.
rationales.power: Pieter "presented the container routing proposal like it was his" and Marc did not challenge it, leaving his status and control publicly undermined.
rationales.conformity: He chose not to "call it out right there," restraining himself in the meeting rather than risking open conflict.

rationales.achievement: "Ran the quarterly review myself... just my numbers, my narrative" frames the meeting as a performance of his competence that landed visibly in the room.
rationales.power: Saying "the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be" shows satisfaction in having authority and command over the group.

rationales.self_direction: He had to keep "refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission," showing him operating under someone else's control instead of his own judgment.
rationales.power: He is fixated on being unable to move "a standard supplier agreement through" despite running the division, highlighting a loss of influence over outcomes that affect his standing.
rationales.conformity: He "thought better of it" and did not go to the CEO, holding himself to the existing chain rather than escalating.

rationales.power: He had "already decided" the reporting structure and, after letting objections run for ten minutes, "moved on," asserting that the decision stayed with him.
rationales.benevolence: When Lotte asked about work, he gave her "fine" and chose not to explain himself, pulling away from a close relationship instead of engaging with her.

rationales.self_direction: Once others started "nodding along with her," he abandoned his own rollout plan and let the room's momentum determine the outcome.
rationales.power: What unsettles him is that "it wasn't my call anymore" and they looked at Sandra "like she was in charge," showing a clear loss of authority.
rationales.conformity: He "ended up agreeing to her phased approach" as the room shifted, yielding to group consensus rather than pressing his own line.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me," showing he did not assert his own judgment or boundaries in the moment.
rationales.power: His own summary, "The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides," makes clear he feels his authority slipping away.
rationales.conformity: Instead of shutting Jeroen down, he "smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion," choosing social smoothness over confrontation.

rationales.power: He "corrected him this time" when Pieter redirected the client conversation, reasserting control over his own account in the moment.
rationales.conformity: The correction "came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed," showing he broke the usual norm of keeping the exchange smooth and unobtrusive.

rationales.power: He says he feels most like himself when people "come to me for direction and I give it clearly," which centers his comfort in being the one with authority.
rationales.benevolence: He patiently "walked through it with her," shared shortcuts, and reassured Lisa that he also struggled at first, directly supporting someone on his team.

rationales.power: Having to "walk him through" numbers he had already submitted made him feel like he had to "audition for the right to present my own results," signaling diminished standing and control.
rationales.conformity: He "complied, obviously" and went through everything line by line, choosing deference to hierarchy over pushing back.
rationales.benevolence: When Lotte asked if he was okay, he said "yes" even though "I wasn't really," shutting down honesty with a close partner.
He "booked a table at De Kas for Lotte's birthday next week," making a concrete plan for someone close to him.

rationales.security: He prefers a "three-year contract extension" that puts "a floor under" the next three years over squeezing for more upside, prioritizing predictability over expansion.
He wanted someone outside the company to know "the ground is solid" and takes comfort in the neighborhood's familiar rhythm, showing how much steadiness matters to him.
rationales.tradition: He lingers on the old desk lamp, paper registrations with "actual stamps," and his father's habit of reporting good news to family first, grounding himself in inherited ways and continuity.

rationales.security: He keeps the reserve fund in place, checks receivables "every Monday morning," and compares reinvestment to pulling bricks from the foundation, all of which show a strong preference for safety buffers.
He postpones the new product line until the Formosa contract "settles in" because he has "seen it take companies apart" when revenue is not locked down.

The possibility of Jia-hao leaving pushes him to reopen the proposal, but his immediate move is still "I would read the proposal again this weekend," a cautious step meant to contain disruption rather than leap into change.
He reads the proposal three times, flags certification delays, and insists "I agreed to look, not to leap," showing sustained caution around uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: He leaves Jia-hao a note saying "Let's talk Monday" because he cannot bear "watching him leave," showing active effort to support the relationship and make room for him.
